questions.question: Which of the following are the three fundamental building blocks of all algorithms?
questions.explanation: Every algorithm is built using sequencing (steps in order), selection (IF/ELSE), and iteration (loops).

questions.question: A student develops an algorithm to count all the words in an essay. They want to modify it to ONLY count words that are longer than 3 letters. Which structure must be added to the algorithm?
questions.explanation: Selection (an IF statement) is needed to check the length of each word before counting it.

questions.question: A robot is in a grid. Which of the following is an example of an iteration structure?
questions.explanation: Iteration refers to repeating a set of instructions, which is what the REPEAT block does.

questions.question: What happens if a REPEAT UNTIL loop's condition is never met?
options: (C) The program enters an infinite loop.
(D) The program automatically changes the condition.
questions.explanation: An infinite loop occurs when the stopping condition of a loop is never satisfied.

questions.question: Which of the following search algorithms requires the list to be sorted before it can be used?
questions.explanation: Binary search works by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half, which is only possible if the data is sorted.

questions.question: In the context of computer science, what is a 'heuristic'?
options: (A) A perfect mathematical formula for solving any problem.
(B) A shortcut or 'rule of thumb' used to find a 'good enough' solution when an exact one takes too long.
(C) A type of computer virus that copies itself.
questions.explanation: Heuristics are used for computationally hard problems to find an approximate solution in a reasonable amount of time.

questions.question: A program takes 60 seconds to run on a single processor. If the same program is run on 3 processors simultaneously and takes 20 seconds, what is the 'speedup'?
questions.explanation: Speedup is calculated as the sequential time divided by the parallel time: 60 / 20 = 3.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes distributed computing?
options: (A) Using multiple cores on a single CPU to run a program.
(B) Running a program across multiple separate computers connected by a network.
(C) Distributing software to users via the cloud.
(D) Using a single computer to store files for multiple users.
questions.explanation: Distributed computing involves multiple computers working together to solve a problem or perform a task.

questions.question: An algorithm is developed to find the highest score in a list of 10,000 students. Which type of search would be most efficient if the list is NOT sorted?
questions.explanation: If a list is not sorted, you must check every item to find the maximum, which is a linear search process.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about parallel computing?
options: (A) It always makes a program run 10 times faster.
(B) It involves breaking a problem into sub-problems that can be solved at the same time.
(C) It is only used for internet-based applications.
(D) It requires the use of at least 100 processors.
questions.explanation: Parallel computing increases efficiency by executing multiple tasks simultaneously.

questions.question: Which of the following problems would most likely require a heuristic solution?
options: (B) Sorting a list of 50 names alphabetically.
(C) Finding the shortest possible route that visits 100 different cities (Traveling Salesperson Problem).
(D) Searching for a specific value in a sorted list.
questions.explanation: The Traveling Salesperson Problem is computationally 'hard,' meaning an exact solution takes an impractical amount of time for large inputs.

options: (B) It will stop repeating and exit the loop.
(C) It will crash into the wall.
(D) It will spin in a circle.
questions.explanation: The loop continues as long as it CAN move forward. Once CAN_MOVE(forward) is False, it stops immediately.
